questionText: The random variable $Z$ has the standard normal distribution, $Z \sim N(0,1)$. Find $P(Z > -1)$, giving your answer to $4$ significant figures.

steps.description: State the distribution and what is required
steps.explanation: We want the area to the right of $z = -1$ under the standard normal curve. The value $-1$ lies one standard deviation to the left of the mean.
The area to the right of a negative value is large (more than half), and it is easiest to rewrite it in terms of a positive value using symmetry.

steps.description: Use the symmetry of the normal curve
steps.explanation: The standard normal curve is symmetric about $0$, so the area to the right of $-1$ equals the mirror-image area to the left of $+1$.

steps.description: Write it as a value of the CDF
steps.explanation: The area to the left of $1$ is exactly the standard normal cumulative distribution function $\Phi$ evaluated at $1$.
The cumulative value at $z = 1$ is $0.8413$ to $4$ significant figures.
So about $84.1\%$ of the standard normal distribution lies above $z = -1$. This is comfortably more than half, as expected for an area starting to the left of the mean.
